*Proof.* By Lemma 1, \(a_2\) is also not a multiple of \(6\) and \(a_2<a_1\). The sequence strictly decreases and consists of positive integers, so it must eventually become less than \(6\). Numbers less than \(6\) have fewer than three proper divisors, hence the iteration cannot continue. ∎

Therefore any valid \(a_1\) must be a multiple of \(6\). Write \(a_1=6k\) with \(k\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\).

**Lemma 3.** For any positive integer \(k\),  
(i) If \(k\) is odd and \(5\nmid k\), then \(f(6k)=6k\).  
(ii) If \(k\) is odd and \(5\mid k\), then \(f(6k)=31k/5\), which is odd and not a multiple of \(6\).  
(iii) If \(k\) is even, then \(f(6k)=13k/2\).

*Proof.*  
- (i) Since \(k\) is odd, \(6k\) is not divisible by \(4\). The largest proper divisor is \(3k\). Because \(5\nmid k\), \(6k\) is not divisible by \(5\); also \(4\nmid6k\). The second largest is \(2k\) (since \(3\mid6k\)), and the third largest is \(k\). Sum \(=6k\).  
- (ii) Write \(k=5t\). Then \(6k=30t\). The proper divisors are \(15t,10t,6t,5t,2t,3t,2t,1\)? Actually the three largest are \(15t,10t,6t\) (because \(6t>5t\)). Sum \(=31t=31k/5\). Since \(k\) is odd, \(t\) is odd, so \(31t\) is odd; hence it is not a multiple of \(2\), and thus not a multiple of \(6\).  
- (iii) Since \(k\) is even, \(6k\) is divisible by \(12\). Write \(6k=12r\) with \(r=k/2\). The divisors \(6r,4r,3r\) are all proper and the three largest (any other divisor is at most \(12r/5<3r\)). Sum \(=13r=13k/2\). ∎

Now we analyse the possible values of \(k\).

- **Case 1:** \(k\) odd and \(5\nmid k\). Then \(a_2=f(6k)=6k\), so the sequence is constant. Since \(a_1\) has at least three proper divisors (it is composite and not a prime power with exponent \(1\)), it is valid. All such \(a_1\) work.

- **Case 2:** \(k\) odd and \(5\mid k\). Then \(a_2=31k/5\) is odd and not a multiple of \(6\). By Corollary 2, the sequence from \(a_2\) is finite. Hence no such \(a_1\) works.

- **Case 3:** \(k\) even. Then \(a_2=13k/2\). For the sequence to be infinite, \(a_2\) must also be a multiple of \(6\); otherwise it would be finite (Corollary 2). Since \(\gcd(13,6)=1\), this forces \(k/2\) to be a multiple of \(6\), i.e. \(k\equiv0\pmod{12}\). Write \(k=12s\); then \(a_1=72s\) and \(a_2=78s\).

Now \(a_2\) is a multiple of \(6\), so we can repeat the analysis with \(a_2\). Set \(k_1=a_2/6=13s\). The behaviour depends on \(k_1\):
  - If \(k_1\) is odd and \(5\nmid k_1\), then \(a_3=a_2\) (fixed point). This happens precisely when \(s\) is odd and \(5\nmid s\). Then \(a_1=72s\) works.
  - If \(k_1\) is odd and \(5\mid k_1\), then the sequence from \(a_2\) is finite (by the same reasoning as Case 2). So no such \(a_1\) works.
  - If \(k_1\) is even, then \(a_3=13k_1/2\) and we need \(a_3\) to be a multiple of \(6\) for the sequence to continue. This requires \(k_1\) to be a multiple of \(12\), i.e. \(13s\equiv0\pmod{12}\). Since \(\gcd(13,12)=1\), this forces \(s\equiv0\pmod{12}\). So \(s=12s_2\), then \(a_1=72\cdot12s_2=864s_2\), \(a_2=78\cdot12s_2=936s_2\), \(a_3=13k_1/2=13\cdot13s/2=169s=169\cdot12s_2=2028s_2\). The process continues.

Iterating this reasoning shows that the only way to eventually reach a fixed point (and thus have an infinite orbit) is that the initial parameter \(k\) can be written as \(k=12^p\cdot s\) with \(s\) odd and \(5\nmid s\). Indeed, if \(k=12^p\cdot s\) with \(s\) odd and \(5\nmid s\), then applying the transformation \(k\mapsto 13k/12\) \(p\) times yields \(k_p=13^p\cdot s\), which is odd and not divisible by \(5\). Hence \(a_{p+1}=6k_p\) is a fixed point, and all intermediate numbers are multiples of \(6\) (hence have at least three proper divisors). The sequence is therefore infinite.

Conversely, if \(k\) is not of that form, then either \(k\) is odd and divisible by \(5\), or \(k\) is even but not a multiple of \(12\), or \(k\) is a multiple of \(12\) but after factoring out all powers of \(12\) the remaining factor is even or divisible by \(5\). In each situation the iteration eventually produces a number that is not a multiple of \(6\), and then by Corollary 2 the sequence becomes finite.

Thus the set of all possible values of \(a_1\) is

\[
\boxed{\{6\cdot 12^p\cdot s \mid p\ge 0,\ s\text{ odd},\ 5\nmid s\}}.
\]

Equivalently, \(a_1=2^{2p+1}\cdot 3^{p+1}\cdot s\) with \(p\ge0\) and \(s\) odd, \(5\nmid s\).

Let \(f(N)\) denote the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\).  For \(N\) with at least three proper divisors, let \(p<q<r\) be the three smallest divisors of \(N\) greater than \(1\).  Then the three largest proper divisors are \(N/p,N/q,N/r\) and
\[
f(N)=\frac{N}{p}+\frac{N}{q}+\frac{N}{r}.
\]

**Lemma 1.**  If \(N\) is not a multiple of \(6\), then \(f(N)<N\).  Moreover, the sequence \(N,f(N),f(f(N)),\dots\) is strictly decreasing until it reaches a number with fewer than three proper divisors (i.e., a prime or a prime square).  Consequently, an infinite sequence can exist only if every term is a multiple of \(6\).

*Proof.*  The smallest divisor \(p\) of \(N\) is either \(2\), \(3\), or a prime \(\ge5\).  
- If \(p=2\) and \(N\) is not a multiple of \(3\), then the three smallest divisors are at least \(2,4,\dots\); one checks that \(f(N)=N/2+N/4+N/r\) (with \(r\ge5\)) is strictly less than \(N\).  (For example, \(N=8\) gives \(f=7\), \(N=10\) gives \(f=8\), etc.)  
- If \(p=3\) and \(N\) is not a multiple of \(2\), then the three smallest divisors are at least \(3,9,\dots\); again \(f(N)<N\) (e.g., \(N=27\) gives \(f=13\), \(N=45\) gives \(f=29\)).  
- If \(p\ge5\), then \(N\) is odd and not divisible by \(3\); the three smallest divisors are at least \(5,25,\dots\) (if \(p=5\) and \(N\) is a prime power) or larger; one checks that \(f(N)<N\) (e.g., \(N=35\) gives \(f=13\), \(N=55\) gives \(f=17\)).  

Thus in all cases \(f(N)<N\).  Since the terms are positive integers, the sequence strictly decreases.  It must eventually stop because the condition “at least three proper divisors” fails for primes and prime squares.  Hence an infinite orbit cannot contain a number that is not a multiple of \(6\).  ∎

Now suppose the sequence is infinite.  By Lemma 1 every term is a multiple of \(6\).  Write the first term as
\[
a_1 = 2^a 3^b c,\qquad a\ge1,\; b\ge1,\; 5\nmid c,\; 3\nmid c.
\]
(The factor \(c\) is not divisible by \(2\) or \(3\).)  The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3\) and the third is:
\[
\begin{cases}
4, & \text{if } a\ge2,\\[2pt]
5, & \text{if } a=1 \text{ and } 5\mid c,\\[2pt]
6, & \text{if } a=1 \text{ and } 5\nmid c.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
f(a_1)=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\frac{13}{12}a_1, & a\ge2,\\[6pt]
\displaystyle\frac{31}{30}a_1, & a=1,\;5\mid c,\\[6pt]
a_1, & a=1,\;5\nmid c.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(a=1\) and \(5\mid c\), then \(f(a_1)\) is not a multiple of \(6\) (it is odd and not divisible by \(3\)).  By Lemma 1 the sequence would then become undefined, so such \(a_1\) cannot give an infinite sequence.  Therefore for an infinite sequence we must have \(a=1\) and \(5\nmid c\); in that case \(f(a_1)=a_1\) and \(a_1\) is a fixed point.

Now consider \(a\ge2\).  Then \(f(a_1)=\frac{13}{12}a_1\).  Write the factorization after \(k\) steps:
\[
a_1^{(k)} = 13^k \cdot 2^{a-2k} \cdot 3^{b-k} \cdot c.
\]
We want to eventually reach a fixed point.  For a number to be a fixed point it must be a multiple of \(6\) and its quotient after removing the factors \(2\) and \(3\) must be odd and not divisible by \(5\).  The exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\) when \(a-2k=1\), i.e. \(k=\frac{a-1}{2}\); this requires \(a\) to be odd.  After \(k=\frac{a-1}{2}\) steps we obtain
\[
a_1^{(k)} = 13^k \cdot 2 \cdot 3^{b-k} \cdot c.
\]
For this number to be a fixed point we need \(b-k\ge 1\) (so that it is divisible by \(3\)) and \(13^k c\) to be odd and not divisible by \(5\).  Since \(c\) is odd and \(13^k\) is odd, the product is odd; the condition \(5\nmid c\) is exactly the requirement that the quotient is not a multiple of \(5\).  The inequality \(b-k\ge 1\) is equivalent to \(b\ge \frac{a+1}{2}\) because \(k=\frac{a-1}{2}\).  Thus the necessary conditions for \(a_1\) to eventually become a fixed point are:
\[
a \text{ is odd},\quad b\ge\frac{a+1}{2},\quad 5\nmid c.
\]

Now set \(a=2k+1\) with \(k\ge0\).  Then \(b\ge k+1\), so we can write \(b=k+1+r\) with \(r\ge0\).  Hence
\[
a_1 = 2^{2k+1}\cdot 3^{k+1+r}\cdot c = 6\cdot 12^k \cdot (3^r c).
\]
The factor \(t=3^r c\) is odd and, because \(c\) is not divisible by \(5\), \(t\) is also not divisible by \(5\).  Conversely, any number of the form \(6\cdot 12^k\cdot t\) with \(k\ge0\) and \(t\) odd, \(5\nmid t\) can be written as \(2^{2k+1}\cdot 3^{k+1}\cdot t'\) after possibly absorbing extra factors of \(3\) into \(t\) (the exponent of \(3\) may be larger than \(k+1\), but the representation still satisfies the conditions with \(c=t\) and \(b=k+1+\text{exponent of }3\text{ in }t\)).

Finally, we verify that such numbers indeed produce an infinite sequence.  For \(a_1=6\cdot 12^k\cdot t\) (with \(t\) odd and \(5\nmid t\)), we have \(a=2k+1\) (odd), \(b\ge k+1\), and \(c=t\).  Applying \(f\) repeatedly, after \(k\) steps we obtain
\[
a_1^{(k)} = 13^k \cdot 2 \cdot 3^{b-k} \cdot t.
\]
Since \(b-k\ge1\) (because \(b\ge k+1\)), this number is a multiple of \(6\) and its quotient after removing \(2\) and \(3\) is \(13^k t\), which is odd and not divisible by \(5\).  Hence \(f(a_1^{(k)}) = a_1^{(k)}\), so the sequence becomes constant from that point onward.  Therefore the sequence is infinite.

Conversely, if \(a_1\) does not satisfy the form \(6\cdot 12^k\cdot t\) with \(t\) odd and \(5\nmid t\), then by the preceding analysis the sequence cannot be infinite.

Thus the set of all possible values of \(a_1\) is
\[
\boxed{\{6\cdot 12^k\cdot t \mid k\in\mathbb{N}_0,\; t\in\mathbb{N},\; t\text{ odd},\; 5\nmid t\}}.
\]

Let \(f(N)\) denote the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\) (if \(N\) has at least three proper divisors). The sequence satisfies \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\) and each term must have at least three proper divisors.

In both cases the sum of the reciprocals is less than \(1\), so \(f(N)<N\). Moreover, if \(N\) is even, \(f(N)\) is odd; if \(N\) is odd, \(f(N)\) is odd. The smallest positive integer with at least three proper divisors is \(6\), so the sequence must eventually become less than \(6\), which is impossible for a term that must have at least three proper divisors. Hence \(a_1\) must be a multiple of \(6\).

**2. Characterization of multiples of \(6\) that work.**  
Write \(a_1=2^a\cdot3^b\cdot u\) with \(\gcd(u,6)=1\). We consider the iteration \(f\) on such numbers.

First, determine \(f(N)\) by the three smallest divisors \(>1\) of \(N\).

- If \(a=1\), then \(N\) is even but not divisible by \(4\). Because \(N\) is a multiple of \(6\), it is divisible by \(2\) and \(3\). The divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3\) and then \(6\) (since \(6\mid N\) and \(4\nmid N\)). The three smallest are \(2,3,6\), so \(f(N)=N/2+N/3+N/6=N\). Thus \(N\) is a fixed point.

- If \(a\ge2\), then \(N\) is divisible by \(4\). The three smallest divisors are \(2,3,4\) (since \(4\mid N\) and \(4<6\)). Hence \(f(N)=N/2+N/3+N/4=13N/12\). For \(f(N)\) to be a multiple of \(6\), \(N\) must be divisible by \(12\), which it is, and the quotient \(M=N/12\) must be even; this requires \(a\ge3\). Moreover, \(f(N)=13M\) is an integer. If \(a=2\), then \(M\) is odd, so \(f(N)\) is odd and not a multiple of \(6\). Thus only \(a\ge3\) can possibly keep the orbit inside the set of multiples of \(6\).

Now analyze the iteration when \(a\ge3\). Let \(N_0=N\) be a multiple of \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\). Write \(N_i=2^{a_i}\cdot3^{b_i}\cdot u_i\) with \(u_i\) coprime to \(6\) and also \(5\nmid u_i\) (since \(5\) never appears). Then:

- If \(a_i=1\), then \(N_i\) is a fixed point: \(f(N_i)=N_i\).  
- If \(a_i\ge2\), then \(f(N_i)=13\cdot2^{a_i-2}\cdot3^{b_i-1}\cdot u_i\). Thus \(a_{i+1}=a_i-2\), \(b_{i+1}=b_i-1\), \(u_{i+1}=13u_i\).

The process continues until \(a_i\) becomes \(1\). For the orbit to stay in the set of multiples of \(6\), we need that for every step before the final one, \(a_i\ge2\) and \(b_i\ge1\). The number of steps \(t\) needed to reduce \(a\) to \(1\) is \(t=(a_0-1)/2\); this must be an integer, so \(a_0\) must be odd. After \(t\) steps we have \(b_t = b_0 - t\). For \(N_t\) to be a multiple of \(6\), we need \(b_t\ge1\), i.e., \(b_0 \ge t+1 = (a_0-1)/2+1 = (a_0+1)/2\). Finally, \(N_t = 2\cdot3^{b_t}\cdot u_t\) with \(u_t = 13^t u_0\) is not divisible by \(5\) (since \(13\) and \(u_0\) are coprime to \(5\)), so it is a fixed point. Hence the sequence becomes constant.

Conversely, if \(a_0\) is odd, \(b_0 \ge (a_0+1)/2\), and \(5\nmid u_0\), then the above process shows that all iterates are multiples of \(6\) and eventually become a fixed point. Thus the sequence is infinite.

Therefore the possible values of \(a_1\) are exactly the positive integers that are multiples of \(6\), not divisible by \(5\), and for which the exponent of \(2\) is odd while the exponent of \(3\) is at least one more than half the exponent of \(2\). In other words,

\[
\boxed{\{2^a\cdot3^b\cdot c \mid a\text{ odd},\; b\ge\frac{a+1}{2},\; \gcd(c,30)=1\}}.
\]

This includes all multiples of \(6\) with \(v_2=1\) and no factor \(5\) (the fixed points) as well as numbers like \(72,216,864,\dots\) but not \(12,24,30,36,48,60,84,\dots\).

We determine all positive integers \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) defined by \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\) (with \(f(N)\) being the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\)) is well‑defined, i.e. every term has at least three proper divisors.

For a composite integer \(N\) with at least three proper divisors, let \(d_2,d_3,d_4\) be its three smallest divisors greater than \(1\). Then the three largest proper divisors are \(N/d_2,N/d_3,N/d_4\), and
\[
f(N)=\frac{N}{d_2}+\frac{N}{d_3}+\frac{N}{d_4}.
\]
Write \(p=d_2\) (the smallest prime factor of \(N\)); then \(d_3\) is either the next prime factor or \(p^2\) if \(p^2\mid N\) and \(p^2\) is smaller than the next prime factor, and \(d_4\) is the third smallest divisor.

---

### 1. Fixed points
Suppose \(f(N)=N\). Then \(p=2\) (otherwise all divisors are odd, so \(f(N)<N\)). Hence \(2\mid N\) and \(3\mid N\) (otherwise the second divisor would be at least \(3\) but then the sum would be smaller than \(N\)). Therefore \(6\mid N\).  
The three smallest divisors are \(2,3\) and the third one, say \(r\). For \(f(N)=N\) we need \(r=6\), i.e. \(N\) is not divisible by \(4\) (otherwise \(4\) would be a divisor and \(4<6\)) and not divisible by \(5\) (otherwise \(5\) would be a divisor and \(5<6\)). Thus the fixed points are exactly the numbers
\[
N=6k,\quad k\ \text{odd},\ 5\nmid k.
\]
For such \(N\) we have \(f(N)=N\), so the sequence becomes constant from the start.

---

### 2. Behaviour under iteration
Let \(N\) be even and divisible by \(3\). Write \(N=6t\). The three smallest divisors are \(2,3\) and \(r\), where \(r\) is the next smallest divisor after \(3\). Since \(4\mid N\) iff \(t\) is even, and \(5\mid N\) iff \(5\mid t\), we have three cases:
* If \(4\nmid N\) and \(5\nmid N\), then \(r=6\) and \(f(N)=N\).
* If \(4\mid N\) and \(5\nmid N\), then \(r=4\) and \(f(N)=13N/12\).
* If \(5\mid N\) and \(4\nmid N\), then \(r=5\) and \(f(N)=31N/30\).

In particular, if \(N\) is not divisible by \(4\) or \(5\), it is a fixed point.  
If \(N\) is divisible by \(4\) but not by \(5\), then \(f(N)=13N/12\). Because \(4\mid N\) and \(6\mid N\), we have \(12\mid N\), so write \(N=12u\). Then \(f(N)=13u\).  
Now \(13u\) is an integer; it is divisible by \(2\) and by \(3\) precisely when \(u\) is a multiple of \(6\) (since \(13\) is coprime to \(2\) and \(3\)). Thus if \(u=6v\) (i.e. \(N=72v\)), then \(f(N)=78v\). For \(78v\) to be a fixed point we need \(v\) odd and \(5\nmid v\); indeed \(78v=6\cdot13v\) and \(13v\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\) when \(v\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\). Hence for
\[
N=72v,\quad v\ \text{odd},\ 5\nmid v,
\]
we have \(a_2=f(N)=78v\) which is a fixed point, so the sequence becomes constant after two steps.

If \(N\) is divisible by \(5\) but not by \(4\), then \(f(N)=31N/30\). Because \(6\mid N\) and \(5\mid N\), we have \(30\mid N\), write \(N=30w\). Then \(f(N)=31w\). But \(186z=6\cdot31z\), and \(31z\) is odd if \(z\) odd, and not divisible by \(5\) if \(z\) not divisible by \(5\). So \(186z\) is a fixed point. ### 3. Verification that these work
If \(a_1=6k\) with \(k\) odd and \(5\nmid k\), then \(a_1\) is not divisible by \(4\) (since the exponent of \(2\) in \(6k\) is exactly \(1\) because \(k\) is odd). Hence \(a_1\) is a fixed point, and the sequence is constant.  
If \(a_1=72u\) with \(u\) odd and \(5\nmid u\), then \(72u\) is divisible by \(8\), so \(4\mid a_1\). Also \(5\nmid a_1\). Thus \(a_2=13\cdot (72u)/12 = 78u\). Now \(78u=6\cdot13u\), and since \(u\) is odd, \(13u\) is odd; also \(5\nmid13u\) because \(5\nmid u\) and \(5\nmid13\). Hence \(a_2\) is a fixed point, and the sequence becomes constant from then on.

### 4. No other values work
We show that any integer not belonging to the two families eventually produces a term that has fewer than three proper divisors, so the sequence cannot be infinite.

* If \(a_1\) is odd, then its smallest prime factor is odd. Since it consists of positive integers, it must eventually become less than \(6\); the only numbers less than \(6\) that have at least three proper divisors are \(6\) itself, but a decreasing sequence cannot reach \(6\) from above if it starts odd. Therefore some term will have fewer than three proper divisors, and the sequence stops.

Let \(f(N)\) be the sum of the three largest proper divisors of a positive integer \(N\). For \(N\) with at least three proper divisors, write the divisors of \(N\) in increasing order: \(1 = d_1 < d_2 < d_3 < \dots < d_k = N\). The three largest proper divisors are \(d_{k-1}, d_{k-2}, d_{k-3}\). Since \(d_i \cdot d_{k+1-i} = N\), we have
\[
d_{k-1} = \frac{N}{d_2},\quad d_{k-2} = \frac{N}{d_3},\quad d_{k-3} = \frac{N}{d_4},
\]
where \(d_2, d_3, d_4\) are the three smallest divisors of \(N\) larger than \(1\). Hence
\[
f(N) = \frac{N}{d_2} + \frac{N}{d_3} + \frac{N}{d_4} = N\left(\frac1{d_2} + \frac1{d_3} + \frac1{d_4}\right). \tag{1}
\]

We are to determine all positive integers \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence \(a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots\) satisfies \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\) and every term has at least three proper divisors.

---

### 1.  The initial value must be a multiple of \(6\)

If \(a_1\) is odd, then \(d_2 \ge 3\) and \(d_3 \ge 5\) (the three smallest divisors are distinct and at least \(3,5,\dots\)). From (1) we get
\[
\frac{f(a_1)}{a_1} = \frac1{d_2}+\frac1{d_3}+\frac1{d_4} < \frac13+\frac15+\frac17 < 1,
\]
so \(f(a_1) < a_1\). The sequence is strictly decreasing, but all terms are required to have at least three proper divisors. The only numbers with fewer than three proper divisors are primes and squares of primes, and a strictly decreasing sequence of positive integers cannot stay inside the set of numbers with at least three proper divisors indefinitely; eventually it must hit a prime or a prime square, which is not allowed. Hence no odd \(a_1\) works.

If \(a_1\) is even but not divisible by \(3\), then \(d_2=2\) and \(d_3 \ge 4\) (because \(3\nmid a_1\)). Moreover, \(d_4 \ge 5\). Thus
\[
\frac{f(a_1)}{a_1} \le \frac12+\frac14+\frac15 = \frac{19}{20} < 1,
\]
so again \(f(a_1) < a_1\). The same decreasing argument shows that such an \(a_1\) cannot produce an infinite admissible sequence. Therefore \(a_1\) must be divisible by \(6\).

---

### 2.  Structure when \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\)

Write \(a_1 = 2^a \cdot 3^b \cdot U\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge1\) and \(U\) coprime to \(6\). Let \(c\) be the smallest divisor of \(a_1\) larger than \(3\). Since \(a_1\) is even and divisible by \(3\), the divisors \(2\) and \(3\) are present. The next divisor is
\[
c = \begin{cases}
4 & \text{if } a\ge2,\\
5 & \text{if } a=1 \text{ and } 5\mid U,\\
6 & \text{if } a=1 \text{ and } 5\nmid U.
\end{cases}
\]
Using (1) we obtain
\[
f(a_1) = a_1\left(\frac12+\frac13+\frac1c\right) = a_1\left(\frac56+\frac1c\right).
\]

---

### 3.  Analysis of the three cases

**Case A:** \(a=1\) and \(5\nmid U\). Then \(c=6\) and \(f(a_1)=a_1\). Hence \(a_1\) is a fixed point. Because it is a multiple of \(6\) (so it has proper divisors \(1,2,3\)), the sequence is constant and infinite. Therefore every such \(a_1\) is admissible.

**Case B:** \(a=1\) and \(5\mid U\). Then \(c=5\) and
\[
f(a_1)=a_1\left(\frac56+\frac15\right)=\frac{31}{30}a_1.
\]
Since \(a_1\) is divisible by \(30\), write \(a_1=30V\) with \(V\) odd (because after removing \(2\cdot3\cdot5\) the remaining factor is odd). Then \(a_2 = 31V\), which is odd. For an odd integer \(N\), the three smallest divisors are at least \(3,5,7\) (or \(3,9,27\) etc.), so as in the odd case we have \(f(N)<N\). Consequently the sequence strictly decreases. Again it must eventually reach a prime or a prime square, contradicting the requirement that every term has at least three proper divisors. Hence no such \(a_1\) works.

**Case C:** \(a\ge2\). Then \(c=4\) and
\[
f(a_1)=a_1\left(\frac56+\frac14\right)=\frac{13}{12}a_1.
\]
Thus \(a_2 = \frac{13}{12}a_1\). Because \(a_1\) is divisible by \(12\) (since \(a\ge2\) and \(b\ge1\)), the process can continue. Define recursively: as long as \(a_n\) is divisible by \(12\), set \(a_{n+1}=\frac{13}{12}a_n\). Write \(a_1 = 2^a \cdot 3^b \cdot U\) with \(U\) coprime to \(6\). After \(t\) steps we obtain
\[
a_{t+1} = 2^{a-2t} \cdot 3^{b-t} \cdot 13^t \cdot U.
\]

For the orbit to stay inside the set of numbers with at least three proper divisors, the process must never become odd or even without a factor \(3\). In particular, we must eventually reach a fixed point (Case A). That is, there must exist a non‑negative integer \(t\) such that
\[
a_{t+1} = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot Z
\]
with \(Z\) odd and \(5\nmid Z\). For this to happen we need
\[
a-2t = 1 \quad\text{and}\quad b-t \ge 1,
\]
and also that the odd part (which is \(13^t \cdot 3^{b-t} \cdot U\)) is not divisible by \(5\). The conditions \(a-2t=1\) and \(b-t\ge1\) are equivalent to
\[
a = 2t+1 \quad\text{(odd)},\qquad b \ge t+1 = \frac{a+1}{2}.
\]
The condition that the odd part is not divisible by \(5\) means that \(5\nmid U\) (since \(13\) and \(3\) are not multiples of \(5\)). Thus the initial value must satisfy:

- \(v_2(a_1)\) is odd,
- \(v_3(a_1) \ge \frac{v_2(a_1)+1}{2}\),
- The integer \(a_1/(2^{v_2(a_1)}3^{v_3(a_1)})\) is not divisible by \(5\).

Conversely, suppose \(a_1\) satisfies these conditions. Set \(t = (v_2(a_1)-1)/2\) (so \(t\ge0\) and \(v_2(a_1)=2t+1\)). Let \(W = a_1/(2^{2t+1}3^{t+1})\), which is an odd integer not divisible by \(5\). Then we can write \(a_1 = 2^{2t+1}\cdot3^{t+1}\cdot W\). The iteration gives
\[
a_{t+1} = 2^{1}\cdot3^{1}\cdot13^t\cdot W = 6\cdot(13^t W).
\]
Since \(13^t W\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\), the number \(6\cdot(13^t W)\) is a fixed point (as shown in Case A). All intermediate terms are multiples of \(6\) and therefore have at least three proper divisors (namely \(1,2,3\)). Hence the sequence is infinite.

---

### 4.  Conclusion

The set of all possible values of \(a_1\) is exactly the set of positive integers \(N\) for which, writing \(N = 2^a \cdot 3^b \cdot U\) with \(U\) coprime to \(6\), we have
\[
a \text{ is odd},\quad b \ge \frac{a+1}{2},\quad 5\nmid U.
\]

Equivalently, every such \(N\) can be expressed uniquely as
\[
N = 2^{2t+1}\cdot 3^{t+1}\cdot Z,
\]
where \(t\ge0\) and \(Z\) is any odd positive integer not divisible by \(5\). These are precisely the initial values for which the sequence \(a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots\) consists entirely of numbers with at least three proper divisors.

\[
\boxed{a_1 = 2^{2t+1}\cdot 3^{t+1}\cdot Z,\quad t\ge0,\ Z\text{ odd},\ 5\nmid Z}
\]

Let \(f(N)\) denote the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\) (if \(N\) has at least three proper divisors). We are to find all positive integers \(a_1\) such that the sequence \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\) is infinite (i.e., every term has at least three proper divisors).

**Fixed points.**  
If \(f(N)=N\) then the three largest proper divisors sum to \(N\). For a composite \(N\) the three largest proper divisors are \(N/p,\;N/q,\;N/r\) where \(p,q,r\) are the three smallest divisors of \(N\) greater than \(1\). The equation \(1/p+1/q+1/r=1\) has the unique solution \((p,q,r)=(2,3,6)\) in distinct positive integers. Hence \(N\) must be divisible by \(6\), and it cannot be divisible by \(4\) or \(5\) (otherwise one of \(p,q,r\) would be \(4\) or \(5\)). Therefore the fixed points are exactly the numbers \(N=6u\) with \(u\) odd and \(5\nmid u\).

**Necessity.**  
Assume the orbit is infinite. Then every term has at least three proper divisors.

- **No term is odd.** Suppose some \(a_n\) is odd. Its smallest prime divisor is an odd prime \(p\ge3\). Because it has at least three proper divisors, it must have at least two distinct prime factors; let \(q\) be the second smallest. The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are at least \(p,q\) and possibly \(p^2\). Their reciprocals sum to at most \(1/3+1/5+1/9=29/45<1\). Hence \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)<a_n\). The sequence strictly decreases. A strictly decreasing sequence of positive integers cannot stay forever inside the set of numbers with at least three proper divisors (the set is well‑ordered), so eventually a term would have fewer than three proper divisors, contradicting infiniteness. Therefore no term is odd; every term is even.

- **No term is divisible by \(5\).** If \(5\mid a_n\) then, as above, the smallest divisor is \(2\). Because \(5\) is a divisor, the three smallest divisors are \(2,5\) and some other divisor. In every case the sum of their reciprocals is \(<1\); consequently \(a_{n+1}<a_n\). Thus the orbit would strictly decrease, and as before it must eventually produce a number with fewer than three proper divisors, a contradiction. Hence \(5\nmid a_n\) for all \(n\).

Now write each term as \(a_n=2^{e_n}\cdot3^{f_n}\cdot c_n\) where \(c_n\) is not divisible by \(2\) or \(3\). From the previous steps we have \(e_n\ge1\) and \(f_n\ge1\) (otherwise the number would be even but not divisible by \(3\); such a number would also force a decrease and eventually a prime, as shown).  

If \(e_n=1\) and \(3\mid a_n\) then the three smallest divisors are \(2,3,6\) and \(f(a_n)=a_n\); this is the fixed‑point case.  
If \(e_n\ge3\) then \(4\mid a_n\) and the three smallest divisors are \(2,3,4\); hence \(f(a_n)=13a_n/12\). In this case the new exponents are \(e_{n+1}=e_n-2\) and \(f_{n+1}=f_n-1\). Therefore the quantity \(e_n+2f_n\) decreases by \(4\) at each step.

Because the orbit is infinite, the numbers never become prime or prime squares, so the process cannot decrease forever. The only way to avoid a strictly decreasing sequence (which would eventually force a term with fewer than three proper divisors) is to stop exactly when \(e_n=1\) and \(f_n=1\) (the fixed point). Hence we must have \(e_1=2k+1\) and \(f_1=k+1\) for some \(k\ge0\). Consequently \(f_1=(e_1+1)/2\). Moreover, the odd part \(c_1\) is never divisible by \(5\) (otherwise the orbit would be finite, as argued). Thus \(a_1=2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot u\) with \(u\) odd and \(5\nmid u\), i.e., \(a_1=6\cdot12^k\cdot u\).

**Sufficiency.**  
If \(a_1=6\cdot12^k\cdot u\) with \(k\ge0\) and \(u\) odd, \(5\nmid u\), then write \(a_1=2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot u\). For \(k\ge1\) the three smallest divisors are \(2,3,4\); the three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/2,\,a_1/3,\,a_1/4\) and their sum is  
\[
f(a_1)=\frac{a_1}{2}+\frac{a_1}{3}+\frac{a_1}{4}= \frac{13a_1}{12}=6\cdot12^{k-1}\cdot(13u).
\]  
Thus \(f(a_1)\) is again of the same form with \(k\) reduced by \(1\). Repeating, after \(k\) steps we reach \(6u\), which is a fixed point because its three smallest divisors are \(2,3,6\). Hence all terms are well‑defined and the orbit is infinite.

Therefore the set of all possible values of \(a_1\) is  
\[
\boxed{\{6\cdot12^{k}\cdot u \mid k\in\mathbb{N}_0,\; u\text{ odd},\; 5\nmid u\}}.
\]

Equivalently, \(a_1\) is a positive integer such that \(6\mid a_1\), \(5\nmid a_1\), and if \(v_2(a_1)=e\) and \(v_3(a_1)=f\) then \(f=(e+1)/2\).

We consider the function \(f(N)=\sum_{d\mid N,\,d<N} d\) for numbers with at least three proper divisors. The sequence is defined by \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\). We determine all \(a_1\) for which the sequence is infinite.

**Lemma 1.** If \(3\nmid N\) then \(f(N)<N\).

*Proof.* Let \(N\) be composite and have at least three proper divisors.  
Case 1: \(N\) is odd. Then all its divisors are odd. The largest proper divisor \(d_1\le N/3\) (since the smallest prime factor is at least \(3\)). The second largest divisor \(d_2\le N/5\) and the third largest \(d_3\le N/7\). Hence  
\(f(N)\le N/3+N/5+N/7=71N/105<N\).  
Case 2: \(N\) is even. Write \(N=2^eM\) with \(M\) odd. If \(e=1\) then \(N=2M\) and the three largest proper divisors are \(M\), \(2\), \(1\). So \(f(N)=M+2+1=M+3\). Since \(M\) is odd and \(M\ge 3\) (otherwise \(N\) would have only two proper divisors), we have \(M+3\le M+M/2\)? Actually \(M+3<N\) for \(N>6\) because \(M+3<2M\) iff \(M>3\). For \(N=10\) we have \(5+3=8<10\); for \(N=14\) we have \(7+3=10<14\); etc. In general, \(M+3<2M\) for \(M>3\) and \(N=2M\) with \(M\ge 5\). For \(M=3\) we would have \(N=6\) which is a fixed point. So the inequality holds.  
If \(e\ge2\) then the three largest proper divisors are \(N/2\), \(N/3\), \(N/4\) (since \(N\) is divisible by \(12\) when \(e\ge2\) and \(3\mid N\)? Actually we need to consider the possibility that \(N\) is not divisible by \(3\) and \(e\ge2\). Then the largest is \(N/2\), the second largest is \(N/4\) because \(N/3\) is not integer and \(N/5\) might be larger than \(N/4\)? Wait, if \(N\) is even and not divisible by \(3\), then the second largest divisor is either \(N/4\) if \(e\ge2\) or \(N/5\) if \(5\mid N\) but \(N/5\) is smaller than \(N/4\)? Actually, we need a uniform bound. For \(N\) even and \(3\nmid N\), the largest divisor is \(N/2\). The next largest divisor is at most \(N/5\)? Not if \(N\) is divisible by \(4\): then \(N/4\) is a divisor and \(N/4> N/5\) for \(N>0\). So the second largest could be \(N/4\) if \(e\ge2\). But then the third largest is at most \(N/5\) if \(5\mid N\) or \(N/7\) etc. So we can bound \(f(N)\le N/2+N/4+N/5=19N/20<N\). So the inequality holds. Hence in all cases \(f(N)<N\). ∎

**Lemma 2.** If \(N\) is odd and has at least three proper divisors, then \(f(N)\) is odd and \(f(N)<N\). Therefore the sequence of odd terms is strictly decreasing and must eventually reach a prime or a prime square, after which the process stops (because a prime or a prime square has fewer than three proper divisors). Consequently, for the sequence to be infinite, no term can be odd.

*Proof.* Odd divisors of \(N\) are odd, so the sum of three odd numbers is odd. The inequality \(f(N)<N\) is given by Lemma 1 for odd \(N\). Hence the odd terms strictly decrease. Since they are positive integers, they cannot be infinite. ∎

From Lemma 2, if the sequence is infinite then every term is even. In particular, \(a_1\) is even. Moreover, if any term is not divisible by \(3\) then by Lemma 1 it is even and not divisible by \(3\) (since it is even and \(3\nmid\) it), and then the next term will be even? Actually, we need to show that if a term is not divisible by \(3\) then the sequence cannot be infinite. Because if a term is even and not divisible by \(3\), then by Lemma 1 we have \(f(N)<N\). However, that alone does not guarantee that the sequence will hit an odd term. But we can argue more directly: Suppose the sequence is infinite. Then all terms are even and all terms must be divisible by \(3\) as well, otherwise we would obtain an odd term eventually. To see this, note that if some term is not divisible by \(3\) then it is even and \(3\nmid\) it. Then by Lemma 1, the next term is smaller. We claim that eventually an odd term appears. This can be proven by induction on the exponent of \(2\) and \(3\), but a simpler way is to use the following: Since the sequence is infinite, it never reaches a number with fewer than three proper divisors. In particular, it never becomes odd. If a term is even and not divisible by \(3\), then we can consider its prime factorization. The map \(f\) on such numbers will reduce the exponent of \(2\) by \(2\) each time we apply the branch for multiples of \(12\), and eventually the exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\), giving a number of the form \(2\cdot\text{odd}\). This number, if it is not divisible by \(3\), will then have an odd image (as shown in the case of \(N=2\cdot\text{odd}\) with \(3\nmid \text{odd}\)), leading to an odd term. So to avoid an odd term, every term must be divisible by \(3\). Hence all terms are multiples of \(6\).

Thus, if the sequence is infinite then \(6\mid a_n\) for all \(n\). In particular, \(6\mid a_1\).

**Lemma 3.** If the sequence is infinite then \(5\nmid a_1\).

*Proof.* Assume \(5\mid a_1\). Since \(6\mid a_1\), we have \(30\mid a_1\)? Not necessarily; \(a_1\) could be \(2\cdot3\cdot5=30\) which is divisible by \(30\). But we can consider the exponent of \(5\) in \(a_1\). Write \(a_1=2^e3^f5^gM\) with \(\gcd(M,30)=1\). Because the sequence is infinite, every term is a multiple of \(6\). In particular, \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\), so \(f\ge1\). Now we examine the forward iteration. The map \(f\) on numbers divisible by \(2\) and \(3\) has two possible branches depending on the exponent of \(2\):

- If \(e\ge2\) then the three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/2\), \(a_1/3\), \(a_1/4\) (since \(a_1\) is divisible by \(12\) when \(e\ge2\) and \(3\mid a_1\)). Hence \(f(a_1)=13a_1/12\). This reduces the exponent of \(2\) by \(2\) and the exponent of \(3\) by \(1\).  
- If \(e=1\) then \(a_1=2\cdot3^f5^gM\). Because \(5\mid a_1\) and \(3\mid a_1\), the three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/2\), \(a_1/3\), \(a_1/5\) (the factor \(a_1/4\) is not integer). Hence \(f(a_1)=31a_1/30\). This reduces the exponent of \(3\) by \(1\) and the exponent of \(5\) by \(1\).

Thus, in either case, the exponent of \(3\) decreases by \(1\) at each step (and the exponent of \(5\) decreases by \(1\) if we are in the second branch). Since the sequence is infinite, the exponent of \(3\) never becomes \(0\) (otherwise we would have a term not divisible by \(3\) and then Lemma 2 would force an odd term). But after at most \(f\) steps the exponent of \(3\) becomes \(0\). This is impossible. Hence \(5\nmid a_1\). ∎

So far we have that \(6\mid a_1\) and \(5\nmid a_1\). Now we analyze the exponent of \(2\) and \(3\) in \(a_1\).

Let \(a_1=2^e3^f c\) where \(c\) is coprime to \(6\) and \(5\nmid a_1\) (so \(5\nmid c\)). Since \(6\mid a_1\), we have \(f\ge1\). We consider two cases:

**Case 1:** \(e=1\). Then \(a_1=2\cdot3^f c\). Because \(3\mid a_1\) and \(2\mid a_1\), but \(4\nmid a_1\), the three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/2=3^f c\), \(a_1/3=2\cdot3^{f-1}c\), and \(a_1/6=3^{f-1}c\)? Actually, we need to determine the top three divisors. For \(N=2M\) with \(M\) odd, the largest divisor is \(M\). The next largest divisor is the largest proper divisor of \(N\) that is less than \(M\). If \(M\) is divisible by \(3\), then \(N/3=2M/3\) is a divisor. Since \(M=3^f c\) and \(f\ge1\), we have \(M/3\) integer, and \(2M/3\) is even. Compare \(M\) and \(2M/3\): \(M>2M/3\) for \(M>0\). The third largest divisor could be \(M/3\) if \(M\) is composite? Actually, we need to find the three largest proper divisors of \(N=2M\). The divisors of \(N\) are all numbers of the form \(2^i d\) with \(d\mid M\). The largest is \(M\) (with \(i=0\)). The next largest is \(2M/3\)? Not necessarily; if \(M\) is a power of \(3\), then \(2M/3\) is a divisor. The third largest could be \(M/3\) or \(2M/9\)? This is similar to the earlier analysis for numbers with exponent of 2 = 1. In fact, we can compute \(f(2M)\) explicitly: It is the sum of the three largest divisors of \(2M\) that are less than \(2M\). The three largest divisors are \(M\), \(2M/3\) (if \(3\mid M\)), and \(M/3\) (if \(M/3\) is an integer and greater than \(2M/9\)? Actually, we need to compare \(2M/3\) and \(M/3\). Since \(2M/3 > M/3\), the second largest is \(2M/3\). The third largest is the next largest divisor. Candidates are \(M/3\) and \(2M/9\)? But \(2M/9\) is smaller than \(M/3\) if \(M>0\)? \(M/3\) vs \(2M/9\): ratio = 3/2, so \(M/3\) is larger. So the third largest is \(M/3\) if \(M\) is divisible by \(3\) and \(M/3\) is a proper divisor. So the three largest are \(M\), \(2M/3\), \(M/3\). Their sum = \(M + 2M/3 + M/3 = 2M = N\). So \(f(N)=N\). Thus, for \(a_1=2\cdot3^f c\) with \(f\ge1\) and \(3\mid a_1\), we have \(f(a_1)=a_1\). This shows that \(a_1\) is a fixed point. So the sequence is constant and infinite. Therefore, any \(a_1\) with \(e=1\) and \(3\mid a_1\) (i.e., \(f\ge1\)) and \(5\nmid a_1\) is valid.

**Case 2:** \(e\ge2\). Then \(a_1\) is divisible by \(4\) and by \(3\), so \(12\mid a_1\). The three largest proper divisors of \(a_1\) are \(a_1/2\), \(a_1/3\), \(a_1/4\) (since \(a_1/4\) is an integer and larger than any divisor of the form \(a_1/5\) if \(5\mid a_1\), because \(1/4>1/5\)). Hence \(f(a_1)=a_1/2+a_1/3+a_1/4=13a_1/12\). This reduces the exponent of \(2\) by \(2\) and the exponent of \(3\) by \(1\). So we have \(a_2=13\cdot2^{e-2}\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot c\). For the sequence to stay in multiples of \(6\) we need that \(a_2\) is also a multiple of \(6\). Since \(13\) is coprime to \(6\), this requires that \(2^{e-2}\) be even (so \(e-2\ge1\)) and \(3^{f-1}\) be divisible by \(3\) (so \(f-1\ge1\)). Hence we must have \(e\ge3\) and \(f\ge2\). If these conditions hold then \(a_2\) is a multiple of \(6\) and we can continue. The process repeats: each step reduces the exponent of \(2\) by \(2\) and the exponent of \(3\) by \(1\). Let \(n\) be the number of steps we can apply this branch. This is possible as long as the exponent of \(2\) is at least \(2\) (so that the number remains divisible by \(4\)? Actually, after \(k\) steps we have exponent \(e-2k\). The branch \(13/12\) is defined only when the number is divisible by \(12\). For the number to be divisible by \(12\) we need exponent of \(2\) at least \(2\) and exponent of \(3\) at least \(1\). So we can apply the branch as long as \(e-2k\ge2\) and \(f-k\ge1\). Thus, the maximum number of steps is \(n=\min\left(\left\lfloor\frac{e-2}{2}\right\rfloor,\, f-1\right)\). However, we also need that the number remains even (which is automatic if the exponent of \(2\) is at least \(1\)) and divisible by \(3\) (which requires \(f-k\ge1\)). After \(n\) steps, we obtain a term \(a_{n+1}=13^n\cdot2^{e-2n}\cdot3^{f-n}\cdot c\). At this point, either \(e-2n=1\) (so the exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\)) or \(f-n=0\) (so the exponent of \(3\) becomes \(0\)) or both. If the exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\) before the exponent of \(3\) becomes \(0\), then the term is of the form \(2\cdot3^{f-n}\cdot13^n\cdot c\). This number is divisible by \(3\) because \(f-n\ge1\). Now, if the exponent of \(2\) is \(1\) and the number is divisible by \(3\), we can compute its image under \(f\). As before, the three largest proper divisors of a number of the form \(2\cdot3^s\cdot K\) (with \(K\) odd and not divisible by \(3\)) are \(3^sK\), \(2\cdot3^{s-1}K\), \(3^{s-1}K\). Their sum equals \(2\cdot3^sK = N\). Hence \(f(N)=N\). So the term becomes a fixed point. For this to happen we need that the number is indeed of that form, i.e., that the exponent of \(2\) is \(1\) and the number is divisible by \(3\). This requires that \(e-2n=1\) and \(f-n\ge1\). Since \(n=(e-1)/2\) (the largest integer such that \(e-2n\ge1\)), we have \(e-2n=1\) automatically. The condition \(f-n\ge1\) becomes \(f\ge n+1 = (e-1)/2+1 = (e+1)/2\). Hence we need \(f\ge (e+1)/2\).

If instead the exponent of \(3\) becomes \(0\) before the exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\), then at some step we have a term with no factor \(3\). Then that term is even and not divisible by \(3\). By Lemma 1, its image under \(f\) will be smaller and eventually we will get an odd term, leading to death. Hence to avoid this, we must have that the exponent of \(3\) never reaches \(0\), i.e., \(f\ge n+1\). So \(f\ge (e+1)/2\).

Thus, for the sequence to be infinite we must have that either \(e=1\) and \(f\ge1\), or \(e\ge3\) odd and \(f\ge (e+1)/2\). Additionally, we already have \(5\nmid a_1\).

Conversely, suppose \(a_1\) satisfies \(6\mid a_1\), \(5\nmid a_1\), and in its prime factorization we have \(a_1=2^e3^f c\) with \(c\) coprime to \(6\), and either \(e=1\) and \(f\ge1\), or \(e\) is odd and \(e\ge3\) and \(f\ge (e+1)/2\). Then we show that the sequence is infinite. If \(e=1\) then as argued \(a_1\) is a fixed point, so the sequence is constant. If \(e\ge3\) and odd, then let \(n=(e-1)/2\). Since \(e\) is odd, \(e-2n=1\). Also, \(f\ge (e+1)/2 = n+1\), so \(f-n\ge1\). Then we have:
\(a_1 = 2^e3^f c\). Apply the \(13/12\) branch repeatedly. For \(k=0,1,\dots,n\) we have \(a_{k+1}=13^k\cdot2^{e-2k}\cdot3^{f-k}\cdot c\). At step \(k=n\) we get \(a_{n+1}=13^n\cdot2^{1}\cdot3^{f-n}\cdot c\). Since \(f-n\ge1\), this number is divisible by \(3\) and has exponent of \(2\) exactly \(1\). Then by the argument for numbers of the form \(2\cdot3^s\cdot K\) with \(K\) coprime to \(6\) and \(3\mid\) it, we have \(f(a_{n+1})=a_{n+1}\). Hence the sequence becomes constant at \(a_{n+1}\). Every term in the orbit is a multiple of \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\), so each term has at least three proper divisors. Thus the sequence is infinite.

Therefore, the set of all possible \(a_1\) is exactly the set of positive integers such that:
- \(6\mid a_1\),
- \(5\nmid a_1\),
- and if we write \(a_1=2^e3^f c\) with \(\gcd(c,6)=1\), then \(e\) is odd and \(f\ge\frac{e+1}{2}\) (if \(e\ge3\)) or \(e=1\) and \(f\ge1\).

Equivalently, \(a_1\) is of the form \(2^{2r+1}\cdot3^s\cdot c\) where \(r\ge0\), \(s\ge r+1\), and \(c\) is a positive integer coprime to \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\). (The case \(r=0\) corresponds to \(e=1\), \(s\ge1\).)

Thus, all possible values of \(a_1\) are those positive integers that are multiples of \(6\), not multiples of \(5\), and whose exponent of \(2\) is odd and whose exponent of \(3\) is at least one more than half the exponent of \(2\) (with the convention that half of \(1\) is \(0.5\), so the condition \(f\ge1\) is needed for \(e=1\)).

\boxed{\text{All positive integers }a_1\text{ such that }6\mid a_1,\ 5\nmid a_1,\text{ and if }a_1=2^e3^f c\text{ with }\gcd(c,6)=1,\text{ then }e\text{ is odd and }f\ge\frac{e+1}{2}.}

We are given an infinite sequence \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) of positive integers, each of which has at least three proper divisors. For each \(n\ge1\), \(a_{n+1}\) is the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(a_n\). Determine all possible values of \(a_1\).

Define the map \(f(N)\) (for \(N>1\) with at least three proper divisors) as the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\). The sequence satisfies \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\) and we require \(a_n\in G\) for all \(n\), where \(G=\{N>1: N\text{ has at least three proper divisors}\}\).

We first analyze \(f\) in terms of the prime factorisation. For a positive integer \(N\) let \(v_2(N)=e\), \(v_3(N)=f\) and let \(N=2^e3^f u\) with \(\gcd(u,6)=1\). We consider four cases.

**Case 1:** \(e=0\) (so \(N\) is odd). The three smallest divisors greater than \(1\) are at most \(3,5,7\) (because the smallest odd prime factor is at least \(3\)). Hence the three largest proper divisors are at most \(N/3,N/5,N/7\) and
\[
f(N)\le N\left(\frac13+\frac15+\frac17\right)=\frac{71}{105}N<N.
\]
Thus \(f(N)<N\).

**Case 2:** \(e=1\) and \(v_3(N)\ge1\). Then \(N\) is even but not divisible by \(4\) and divisible by \(3\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3,6\) (since \(3<4\) and \(6\) divides \(N\)). The three largest proper divisors are \(N/2,N/3,N/6\) and
\[
f(N)=\frac{N}{2}+\frac{N}{3}+\frac{N}{6}=N.
\]
So \(f(N)=N\).

**Case 3:** \(e=1\) and \(v_3(N)=0\). Then \(N\) is even but not divisible by \(4\) and not divisible by \(3\). Let \(p\) be the smallest odd prime factor of \(N\); \(p\ge5\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,p,2p\) (because \(p>4\)). Hence the three largest proper divisors are \(N/2,N/p,N/(2p)\) and
\[
f(N)=N\left(\frac12+\frac1p+\frac1{2p}\right)=N\frac{p+3}{2p}.
\]
Since \(p\ge5\), \(\frac{p+3}{2p}\le\frac{8}{10}=\frac45<1\), so \(f(N)<N\).

**Case 4:** \(e\ge2\) and \(v_3(N)\ge1\). Then \(N\) is divisible by \(4\) and by \(3\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3,4\) (because \(3<4\)). The three largest proper divisors are \(N/2,N/3,N/4\) and
\[
f(N)=\frac{N}{2}+\frac{N}{3}+\frac{N}{4}=\frac{13}{12}N>N.
\]

**Case 5:** \(e\ge2\) and \(v_3(N)=0\). Then \(N\) is divisible by \(4\) but not by \(3\). Let \(p\) be the smallest odd prime factor of \(N\); \(p\ge5\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,4,p\) (since \(p>4\)). The three largest proper divisors are \(N/2,N/4,N/p\) and
\[
f(N)=N\left(\frac12+\frac14+\frac1p\right)=N\left(\frac34+\frac1p\right).
\]
Because \(p\ge5\), \(\frac34+\frac1p\le\frac34+\frac15=\frac{19}{20}<1\); thus \(f(N)<N\).

We have therefore characterised the behaviour of \(f\):
- If \(N\) is odd, then \(f(N)<N\).
- If \(N\) is even and divisible by \(3\) but not by \(4\), then \(f(N)=N\) (fixed point).
- If \(N\) is even and divisible by \(3\) and by \(4\), then \(f(N)=\frac{13}{12}N>N\).
- In all other cases (i.e. when \(3\nmid N\)), we have \(f(N)<N\).

Now consider an infinite orbit \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) with all terms in \(G\). The first term \(a_1\) cannot be odd, because if it were odd then \(a_2=f(a_1)<a_1\) and the sequence would be strictly decreasing. Since it is a positive integer sequence, it must eventually become \(1\) or a prime, neither of which belongs to \(G\) (a prime has only one proper divisor; \(1\) has no proper divisors). Hence the orbit would be finite, contradiction. Therefore \(a_1\) is even.

If \(a_1\) were not divisible by \(3\), then either \(v_2(a_1)=1\) (and then \(a_2< a_1\)) or \(v_2(a_1)\ge2\) (and then also \(a_2<a_1\)). So again the sequence would be strictly decreasing, eventually leaving \(G\). Thus we must have \(3\mid a_1\). Consequently \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\).

Now write \(a_1=2^e3^f u\) with \(\gcd(u,6)=1\). We have \(e\ge1\), \(f\ge1\). There are two possibilities:

- **Subcase \(e=1\).** Then \(a_1\) is not divisible by \(4\) and \(3\mid a_1\). By Case 2, \(f(a_1)=a_1\), so \(a_2=a_1\). The sequence is constant; hence it is infinite. Thus every number of the form \(2\cdot3^f u\) with \(f\ge1\) and \(\gcd(u,6)=1\) is a valid starting value.

- **Subcase \(e\ge2\).** Then \(a_1\) is divisible by \(4\) and by \(3\). By Case 4, \(f(a_1)=\frac{13}{12}a_1>a_1\). So the sequence increases. Write \(a_1=2^e3^f u\) with \(e\ge2\), \(f\ge1\), \(\gcd(u,6)=1\). Then
\[
a_2=f(a_1)=13\cdot2^{e-2}\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot u.
\]
The new valuations are \(v_2(a_2)=e-2\), \(v_3(a_2)=f-1\) and the odd part is \(13u\). Repeating the same argument, we see that as long as \(v_2\ge2\) and \(v_3\ge1\) the map multiplies the number by \(\frac{13}{12}\). This reduces the exponent of \(2\) by \(2\) and the exponent of \(3\) by \(1\) each step. After \(t\) steps we have
\[
a_{t+1}=13^t\cdot2^{e-2t}\cdot3^{f-t}\cdot u.
\]
For the sequence to become constant at a fixed point we need that after a finite number of steps we obtain a number with \(v_2=1\) and \(v_3\ge1\) (so that it is a fixed point). The exponent of \(2\) becomes \(1\) when \(e-2t=1\), i.e. \(t=\frac{e-1}{2}\). For this to be an integer we need \(e\) odd. At that moment the exponent of \(3\) is \(f-t = f-\frac{e-1}{2}\). To have a fixed point we need this to be at least \(1\), i.e.
\[
f\ge\frac{e+1}{2}.
\]
Moreover the remaining odd part is \(13^t u\). For the fixed point to be in \(G\) we need it not to be divisible by \(5\); since \(13^t\) is not a multiple of \(5\), this is equivalent to \(5\nmid u\). Thus the necessary conditions for the orbit to be infinite are:
\[
e\text{ odd},\quad f\ge\frac{e+1}{2},\quad 5\nmid u.
\]
Conversely, if these conditions hold, then the process continues for \(t=\frac{e-1}{2}\) steps, and at that moment we obtain
\[
a_{t+1}=2\cdot3^{f-\frac{e-1}{2}}\cdot13^{\frac{e-1}{2}}\cdot u.
\]
Since \(f-\frac{e-1}{2}\ge1\), this number is divisible by \(6\) and has \(v_2=1\), so it is a fixed point. Hence from that term onward the sequence is constant, and the orbit is infinite.

Therefore the possible values of \(a_1\) are exactly those positive integers \(N\) such that when written as \(N=2^e3^fu\) with \(\gcd(u,6)=1\), we have
\[
e\text{ odd},\quad f\ge\frac{e+1}{2},\quad 5\nmid u.
\]
This can be rewritten as
\[
a_1=2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot w,
\]
where \(k\) is a non‑negative integer and \(w\) is a positive integer with \(\gcd(w,2)=1\) and \(5\nmid w\).

\boxed{\text{All positive integers of the form }2^{2k+1}3^{k+1}w\text{ with }k\ge0\text{ and }w\text{ coprime to }2\text{ and not divisible by }5.}

We will determine all possible initial values \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence exists. Let \(f(N)\) be the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\). The condition that each term has at least three proper divisors implies that \(N\) is not \(1\), prime, or a square of a prime. We analyze the dynamics of \(f\).

First, we note the following classification for numbers with at least three proper divisors.

**Lemma 1.** Let \(N\) be a composite integer with at least three proper divisors. Denote by \(p\) the smallest prime divisor of \(N\) and by \(a_3<a_4\) the second and third smallest divisors larger than \(1\). Then the three largest proper divisors are \(N/p\), \(N/a_3\), and \(N/a_4\) (the last one is \(1\) when \(N\) has exactly four divisors). Hence
\[
f(N)=N/p+N/a_3+N/a_4.
\]

**Lemma 2.** If \(N\) is not a multiple of \(6\), then \(f(N)<N\) and the orbit under \(f\) never contains a multiple of \(6\).  
*Proof.* Suppose \(3\nmid N\). Then every divisor of \(N\) is not a multiple of \(3\). The numbers \(N/p\), \(N/a_3\), and \(N/a_4\) are all not multiples of \(3\). Their sum can be a multiple of \(3\) only if the three residues are all \(1\) or all \(2\) modulo \(3\). However, in the case \(N\) is odd, all divisors are odd, so each term is odd; then \(f(N)\) is odd, and thus cannot be a multiple of \(6\). If \(N\) is even but not a multiple of \(3\), we consider two subcases. If \(4\mid N\), then \(N=4k\) with \(k\) not a multiple of \(3\). Then \(f(N)=3k+N/a_4\). Since \(3k\) is a multiple of \(3\) and \(N/a_4\) is not a multiple of \(3\) (because \(3\nmid N\)), we have \(f(N)\equiv N/a_4\pmod{3}\), which is not \(0\). Therefore \(f(N)\) is not a multiple of \(3\). Also \(f(N)\) is even? Actually \(3k\) is odd if \(k\) odd, even if \(k\) even; but \(N/a_4\) is an integer. However, from the expression we can see that \(f(N)\) is odd when \(k\) is odd (since \(3k\) odd and \(N/a_4\) even? In examples we saw odd; but we don't need parity, just that it's not a multiple of \(6\) because it's not divisible by \(3\)). If \(4\nmid N\), then \(N\) is twice an odd number; then \(N/2\) is odd, and \(N/a_3\) and \(N/a_4\) are also odd (since they are divisors of an even number not divisible by \(4\), they are odd). Hence \(f(N)\) is the sum of three odd numbers, which is odd. Thus \(f(N)\) is odd, not divisible by \(2\). Therefore \(f(N)\) cannot be a multiple of \(6\) in any case. Consequently, the orbit starting from a non‑multiple of \(6\) never contains a multiple of \(6\). Moreover, for such \(N\) we have \(f(N)<N\) (this is straightforward by considering the values of the terms). Hence the sequence is strictly decreasing until it becomes a prime or a square of a prime, at which point the process stops. So any number not divisible by \(6\) cannot have an infinite orbit. ∎

Now we study the numbers that are multiples of \(6\). Write \(N=6x\). The smallest prime divisor is \(2\) and the second smallest divisor is \(3\). The third smallest divisor \(a_4\) is the smallest divisor greater than \(3\) that divides \(N\). It can be \(4\), \(5\), or \(6\). Therefore
\[
f(N)=
\begin{cases}
\frac{13}{12}N & \text{if }4\mid N,\\[4pt]
\frac{31}{30}N & \text{if }5\mid N\text{ but }4\nmid N,\\[4pt]
N & \text{if }4\nmid N\text{ and }5\nmid N.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the fixed points of \(f\) are exactly the multiples of \(6\) that are not divisible by \(4\) and not divisible by \(5\). Denote this set by \(F\). So
\[
F=\{6k : k\text{ is odd and }5\nmid k\}.
\]

**Lemma 3.** A number \(N\) has an infinite orbit under \(f\) if and only if it can be written as \(N=12^n\cdot m\) with \(n\ge0\) and \(m\in F\).  
*Proof.* We show that the set \(G\) of numbers with infinite orbit satisfies the recursive definition
\[
G = F \cup \{12u : u\in G\},
\]
and that every element of \(G\) is of the form \(12^n\cdot m\) with \(m\in F\).  
If \(N\in F\), then \(f(N)=N\), so the orbit is constant; thus \(N\in G\).  
If \(N\in G\) and \(4\mid N\), then \(N=12u\) for some integer \(u\). Because \(N\) is a multiple of \(12\), we have \(f(N)=13u\). Since \(N\) is in \(G\), the orbit is infinite, so \(f(N)\) must also be in \(G\). Hence \(13u\in G\). As we argued in Lemma 2, if a number is in \(G\) then it must be a multiple of \(6\). Therefore \(13u\) is a multiple of \(6\), which forces \(u\) to be a multiple of \(6\) (because \(13\) is coprime to \(6\)). Write \(u=6v\). Then \(N=12\cdot6v=72v\) and \(f(N)=78v\). Since \(78v\in G\), we can apply the same reasoning. By induction, after \(n\) steps we obtain a number that is in \(F\). So every element of \(G\) is of the form \(12^n\cdot m\) with \(m\in F\). Conversely, if \(N=12^n\cdot m\) with \(m\in F\), then we prove by induction that \(f^k(N)\) eventually becomes constant. Indeed, let \(m=6t\) with \(t\) odd and \(5\nmid t\). Then
\[
f(N)=13\cdot12^{n-1}\cdot m = 13\cdot12^{n-1}\cdot6t = 6\cdot12^{n-1}\cdot13t.
\]
Now \(13t\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\), so \(6\cdot13t\in F\). Hence \(f(N)\) is a multiple of \(12\) times a fixed point. Repeating, after \(n\) steps we reach \(f^n(N)=13^n\cdot6t\), which belongs to \(F\) (because \(13^n\) is odd and coprime to \(2\) and \(3\), so \(13^n\cdot6t\) is not divisible by \(4\) and not divisible by \(5\)). Thus \(f^{n+1}(N)=f(13^n\cdot6t)=13^n\cdot6t\) since it's a fixed point. So the orbit becomes constant. Hence \(N\in G\). ∎

Therefore the possible values of \(a_1\) are precisely all positive integers that can be written as
\[
a_1 = 12^n \cdot m,\qquad n\ge0,\; m\in F.
\]
Since \(F\) consists of numbers \(6k\) with \(k\) odd and not divisible by \(5\), we have
\[
a_1 = 12^n \cdot 6k = 6\cdot12^n\cdot k,
\]
where \(k\) is odd and \(5\nmid k\). Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{a_1 = 6\cdot(12)^n\cdot t,\quad n\ge0,\; t\text{ odd},\; 5\nmid t}.
\]

We may also express this as
\[
a_1 = 2^{2n+1}\cdot3^{n+1}\cdot t,\quad n\ge0,\; t\text{ odd},\; 5\nmid t.
\]

This characterizes all possible initial values \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence exists. ∎

We prove that the only possible values of \(a_1\) are those of the form \(6m\) with \(m\) odd and \(5\nmid m\), or of the form \(72m\) with \(m\) odd and \(5\nmid m\).

Let \(S(N)\) denote the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\). The sequence is defined by \(a_{n+1}=S(a_n)\) for all \(n\ge 1\), and each \(a_n\) has at least three proper divisors.

**Lemma 1.** If \(N\) is not a multiple of \(6\), then \(S(N)<N\).

*Proof.* Let \(d_2<d_3<d_4\) be the three smallest divisors of \(N\) greater than \(1\). Then \(S(N)=N/d_2+N/d_3+N/d_4\). Consider the smallest prime factor \(p\) of \(N\).

- If \(p=2\) and \(3\nmid N\), then \(d_2=2\). The next smallest divisor is at least \(4\) (if \(4\mid N\)) or at least \(5\) (if \(4\nmid N\)). In any case, the three smallest divisors are at least \(2,4,8\) (the sum is maximized when they are exactly \(2,4,8\)). Hence \(S(N)\le N/2+N/4+N/8=7N/8<N\).

- If \(p=3\) and \(2\nmid N\), then \(N\) is odd and divisible by \(3\). The three smallest divisors are at least \(3,5,9\) (since the smallest possible \(d_3\) is \(5\) if \(5\mid N\), otherwise larger, and the third smallest is at least \(9\) if \(9\mid N\) or \(15\) otherwise; the worst case for the sum occurs when the divisors are as small as possible, i.e., \(3,5,9\)? Actually we need a uniform bound: For any such \(N\), we have \(S(N)\le N/3+N/5+N/15 = 3N/5 < N\). This bound is valid because the maximum sum is attained when the three smallest divisors are \(3,5,15\) (if \(15\mid N\)) or \(3,5,9\) (if \(9\mid N\) and \(5\mid N\)? Let's check the maximum possible value of \(N/3+N/d_3+N/d_4\). The smallest possible \(d_2\) is \(3\). The next smallest possible \(d_3\) is \(5\) (if \(5\mid N\)); otherwise it is at least \(7\). The third smallest possible \(d_4\) is at least \(9\) (if \(9\mid N\)) or \(15\) (if \(5\mid N\) and \(3\mid N\)). In all cases, we have \(N/d_3+N/d_4 \le N/5+N/9\) or \(N/5+N/15\) etc., but the sum \(N/3+N/5+N/9\) is larger than \(N/3+N/5+N/15\) because \(N/9>N/15\). However, the bound \(N/3+N/5+N/15\) is not always an upper bound if the third divisor is \(9\) (since \(N/9 > N/15\)). We need a bound that holds for all \(N\). Observe that if \(N\) is odd and divisible by \(3\), then the three smallest divisors are at least \(3,5,9\) (if \(5\mid N\) and \(9\mid N\)? Actually if both \(5\) and \(9\) divide \(N\), then \(3,5,9\) are divisors; if \(5\nmid N\) but \(9\mid N\), then divisors are \(3,9,?\) and the third is at least \(15\)? Let's do a more systematic approach: Since \(2\nmid N\), all divisors are odd. The smallest divisor is \(3\). The next smallest divisor is either \(5\) (if \(5\mid N\)) or \(7\) (if \(7\mid N\)), etc., but in any case it is at least \(5\). The third smallest divisor is at least the next odd prime factor or \(9\) if \(9\mid N\)? Actually if \(N\) is divisible by \(9\), then \(9\) is a divisor. So the third smallest could be \(9\) if \(5\nmid N\), or \(15\) if \(5\mid N\) and \(3\mid N\), etc. To get a universal bound, we can use the fact that \(N/3+N/d_3+N/d_4 \le N/3+N/5+N/9\)? But \(N/5\) may not be integer if \(5\nmid N\). However, we can consider the maximum possible value of the sum over all \(N\) with \(2\nmid N\) and \(3\mid N\). The sum is maximized when the three smallest divisors are as small as possible. The smallest possible \(d_2\) is \(3\). The smallest possible \(d_3\) is \(5\) (since \(5\) is the next odd prime). The smallest possible \(d_4\) is \(9\) (if \(9\mid N\)) or \(15\)? But if \(9\mid N\) and \(5\mid N\), then \(d_3=5\), \(d_4=9\). So the sum becomes \(N/3+N/5+N/9\). Since \(N=3^a\cdot m\) with \(a\ge2\) and \(m\) not divisible by \(3\), we have \(N/3=3^{a-1}m\), \(N/5\) integer only if \(5\mid N\), and \(N/9=3^{a-2}m\). The sum is \(3^{a-1}m+3^{a-2}m+N/5\)? This is not a simple fraction of \(N\). To avoid case analysis, we can instead note that if \(N\) is not a multiple of \(6\), then either \(2\nmid N\) or \(3\nmid N\). If \(3\nmid N\), we already have a bound. If \(2\nmid N\), then the smallest divisor is at least \(3\). Then we can bound \(S(N) \le N/3+N/5+N/15 = 3N/5 < N\) because the smallest possible \(d_3\) is \(5\) and the smallest possible \(d_4\) is \(15\)? But if \(N\) is divisible by \(3\) and \(5\), then \(15\) is a divisor, but we also have the possibility of \(9\) being a divisor. However, if \(N\) is divisible by \(3\) and \(5\) and also by \(9\), then \(d_3=5\), \(d_4=9\), and \(N/3+N/5+N/9 > N/3+N/5+N/15\). So the bound \(3N/5\) is not an upper bound in that case; it's a lower bound. So we need to use a different approach.

Better: Use a simple case analysis based on the smallest prime factor \(p\). For \(N\) not a multiple of \(6\), we have:

- If \(p=2\) and \(3\nmid N\): Then \(d_2=2\), \(d_3\ge4\), \(d_4\ge8\). So \(S(N)\le N/2+N/4+N/8=7N/8<N\).

- If \(p=3\) and \(2\nmid N\): Then \(N\) is odd and divisible by \(3\). The three smallest divisors are at least \(3,5,9\) (since the next smallest odd prime after \(3\) is \(5\), and the third smallest is at least \(9\) because \(3^2=9\) is the smallest odd square). Indeed, the divisors are all odd, so the three smallest positive divisors >1 are at least \(3,5,9\). Therefore \(S(N)\le N/3+N/5+N/9\). But \(N/3+N/5+N/9 = N*(15+9+5)/45 = 29N/45 < N\). So \(S(N)<N\). This bound holds because if \(N\) has a divisor \(5\), then \(d_3=5\), and if it has a divisor \(9\), then \(d_4=9\); if \(N\) does not have \(5\) or \(9\), the sum is even smaller. So \(S(N)\le 29N/45<N\).

- If \(p\ge5\): Then \(N\) is odd and not divisible by \(3\). The three smallest divisors are at least \(5,7,35\)? Actually the smallest possible \(d_2\) is \(5\), the next is at least \(7\) (if \(7\mid N\)), and the third is at least \(25\)? But the maximum sum occurs when the three smallest divisors are as small as possible: \(5,7,35\) gives \(N/5+N/7+N/35 = N*(7+5+1)/35 = 13N/35 < N\). So \(S(N)<N\).

Thus Lemma 1 holds. (The proof is concise; we can present it as above.)

Now, suppose the sequence \(a_n\) is defined for all \(n\ge1\). If some term \(a_k\) is not a multiple of \(6\), then by Lemma 1, \(a_{k+1}=S(a_k)<a_k\). Moreover, the sequence from that point onward is strictly decreasing (if a term is not a multiple of \(6\), the next is smaller; if the next is still not a multiple of \(6\), it continues decreasing; if it becomes a multiple of \(6\), we will consider later). Because the terms are positive integers, the sequence must eventually reach a term that has at most two proper divisors (i.e., a prime or a square of a prime). At that point the sequence cannot be continued because the definition requires at least three proper divisors. Hence, for an infinite sequence to exist, every term must be a multiple of \(6\).

So we have: \(6\mid a_n\) for all \(n\).

Write \(a_n=6n_n\). Then, as derived, \(S(a_n)=
\begin{cases}
6n_n, & \text{if } n_n \text{ is odd and } 5\nmid n_n;\\
\frac{31}{5}n_n, & \text{if } n_n \text{ is odd and } 5\mid n_n;\\
\frac{13}{2}n_n, & \text{if } n_n \text{ is even}.
\end{cases}
\)

(Justification: When \(n_n\) is odd, the three smallest divisors of \(6n_n\) are \(2,3,6\) if \(5\nmid n_n\) (since \(5\) does not divide \(n_n\) and \(2,3\) divide, so \(6\) is the third smallest; when \(5\mid n_n\), the smallest divisors are \(2,3,5\); when \(n_n\) is even, the smallest divisors are \(2,3,4\) because \(4\mid 6n_n\).)

Now, for the sequence to be infinite, each \(a_{n+1}=S(a_n)\) must also be a multiple of \(6\). So:

- If \(n_n\) is odd and \(5\mid n_n\), then \(a_{n+1}=31n_n/5\) is odd (since \(n_n\) is odd and divisible by \(5\), so \(n_n=5t\) with \(t\) odd; then \(a_{n+1}=31t\), odd). Hence \(a_{n+1}\) is not a multiple of \(6\), contradiction. Therefore \(n_n\) cannot be odd and divisible by \(5\).

- If \(n_n\) is odd and \(5\nmid n_n\), then \(a_{n+1}=6n_n\) which is a multiple of \(6\), and the sequence becomes constant from that point. Thus such \(n_n\) are allowed.

- If \(n_n\) is even, then \(a_{n+1}=13n_n/2\). For this to be a multiple of \(6\), we need \(13n_n/2\) divisible by \(6\). Since \(\gcd(13,6)=1\), this is equivalent to \(n_n/2\) divisible by \(6\), i.e., \(n_n\) is a multiple of \(12\). Write \(n_n=12k\). Then \(a_{n+1}=78k=6\cdot(13k)\). For this to be a fixed point, we need that \(13k\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\) (so that \(a_{n+1}\) becomes a fixed point). Since \(13\) is odd, \(13k\) is odd iff \(k\) is odd. Also \(5\nmid 13k\) iff \(5\nmid k\). Hence \(k\) must be odd and not divisible by \(5\).

Thus, the only possible initial values \(a_1\) that can lead to an infinite sequence are:

- \(a_1=6n\) with \(n\) odd and \(5\nmid n\) (then the sequence is constant);
- \(a_1=6n\) with \(n\) even, \(n=12k\), and \(k\) odd, \(5\nmid k\) (so \(a_1=72k\)).

Now, we verify that these indeed give infinite sequences. For the first family, \(a_1=6n\) with \(n\) odd and \(5\nmid n\): Then \(a_2=6n\), so the sequence is constant; since \(a_1\) has at least three proper divisors by hypothesis, the sequence is well-defined. For the second family, \(a_1=72k\) with \(k\) odd and \(5\nmid k\): Then \(a_2=78k\). Now, \(78k=6\cdot(13k)\) and \(13k\) is odd and not divisible by \(5\) (because \(k\) odd and \(5\nmid k\)), so \(a_2\) is a fixed point of the recurrence; thus from \(a_2\) onward the sequence is constant. Again, \(a_1\) has at least three proper divisors (for \(k=1\), \(72\) has many; for any such \(k\), \(72k\) is composite and not a square of a prime, so it has at least three proper divisors). Hence the sequence exists.

Therefore, all possible values of \(a_1\) are exactly the positive integers of the form \(6m\) with \(m\) odd and \(5\nmid m\), or of the form \(72m\) with \(m\) odd and \(5\nmid m\). This completes the determination.

\boxed{a_1 = 6m\text{ with }m\text{ odd and }5\nmid m,\text{ or }a_1 = 72m\text{ with }m\text{ odd and }5\nmid m.}

We define \(f(N)\) to be the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\). The sequence satisfies \(a_{n+1}=f(a_n)\). The condition is that each \(a_n\) has at least three proper divisors.

We first show that \(a_1\) must be even. If \(a_1\) is odd and has at least three proper divisors, then its smallest prime divisor is odd. The three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/p_2\), \(a_1/p_3\), \(a_1/p_4\) where \(p_2,p_3,p_4\) are the three smallest divisors \(>1\). Since all these quotients are odd, \(f(a_1)\) is odd. Moreover, \(p_2\ge3\), \(p_3\ge5\), \(p_4\ge7\) (or if \(a_1\) has exactly three proper divisors, then \(a_1=p_2p_3\) and \(f(a_1)=p_2+p_3+1\), which is \(<p_2p_3\) for \(p_2,p_3\ge3\)). Thus \(f(a_1)<a_1\). The sequence is strictly decreasing and positive, so it must eventually reach a number with fewer than three proper divisors, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence \(a_1\) cannot be odd.

Now suppose \(a_1\) is even but not divisible by \(3\). Then \(a_1\) has no factor \(3\). Let \(p=2\) be the smallest prime divisor. The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2\), then some \(q\ge4\), then \(r\ge5\) (if \(a_1\) is divisible by \(4\)) or \(q\ge5\) (if \(a_1\) is exactly \(2\) times an odd number). In both cases, the three largest proper divisors are \(a_1/2\), \(a_1/q\), \(a_1/r\), all integers. One can check that \(a_1/2\) is not divisible by \(3\) because \(a_1\) has no factor \(3\), and \(a_1/q\), \(a_1/r\) are also not divisible by \(3\) since \(q,r\) are divisors of \(a_1\) and \(a_1\) has no factor \(3\). Hence \(f(a_1)\) is not divisible by \(3\). Also, \(1/2+1/q+1/r<1\) (since \(q\ge4\), \(r\ge5\)), so \(f(a_1)<a_1\). Again the sequence decreases and never becomes divisible by \(3\), so it eventually falls below \(6\) and cannot have at least three proper divisors, a contradiction. Therefore \(a_1\) must be divisible by \(3\).

Thus \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\). Write its prime factorization as \(a_1=2^e\cdot3^f\cdot n\) where \(n\) is coprime to \(6\) (i.e., \(5\nmid n\) but \(n\) may have other prime factors). Since \(a_1\) has at least three proper divisors, \(e\ge1\) and \(f\ge1\).

We now determine necessary and sufficient conditions on \(e,f,n\) for the existence of an infinite sequence with each term having at least three proper divisors.

**Necessity.** Assume the sequence is infinite and each \(a_n\) has at least three proper divisors. Then \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\). We analyze the first step.

- If \(e=1\), then \(a_1=2\cdot3^f\cdot n\). The divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3\), and then the next divisor after \(3\) could be \(5\) if \(5\mid a_1\), otherwise \(6\). If \(5\mid a_1\), then \(a_1=2\cdot3^f\cdot5\cdot t\) with \(t\) not divisible by \(5\) and coprime to \(6\). Then \(f(a_1)=a_1/2+a_1/3+a_1/5 = 3^f\cdot5\cdot t+2\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot5\cdot t+2\cdot3^f\cdot t = 31\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot t\). This is odd and \(5\nmid\) it? Actually \(31\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot t\) is odd, and it would not be divisible by \(3\) because \(3\nmid31\) and \(3\nmid t\)? Wait, \(t\) is coprime to \(6\), so \(3\nmid t\). Thus \(f(a_1)\) is odd and not divisible by \(3\). As argued earlier, an odd number cannot survive because its forward orbit would strictly decrease and eventually become a prime or a prime square. Hence \(5\nmid a_1\) is required for infinite sequence. So \(5\nmid n\). Then the three smallest divisors are \(2,3,6\), and \(f(a_1)=a_1\). Thus \(a_1\) is a fixed point.

- If \(e\ge3\), then \(a_1\) is divisible by \(12\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3,4\), so
  $$a_2 = f(a_1)=a_1/2+a_1/3+a_1/4 = 13\cdot2^{e-2}\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot n.$$
  Now \(a_2\) must also be in \(S\). Since the sequence is infinite, the same reasoning applies to \(a_2\). Write \(a_2\) in the same form: \(a_2=2^{e-2}\cdot3^{f-1}\cdot(13n)\). For \(a_2\) to be a multiple of \(6\), we need \(e-2\ge1\) and \(f-1\ge1\) (i.e., \(e\ge3\), \(f\ge2\)). Moreover, by the same argument as for \(a_1\), we must have that the exponent of \(2\) in \(a_2\) is odd and the exponent of \(3\) satisfies the condition. This suggests an induction on \(e\). More formally, suppose the statement holds for all numbers with exponent of \(2\) smaller than \(e\). For \(a_1\) with exponent \(e\), we have \(a_2\) with exponent \(e-2\). For \(a_2\) to have an infinite forward orbit, by the induction hypothesis we need \(e-2\) odd and \(f-1 \ge \frac{(e-2)-1}{2}+1 = \frac{e-1}{2}\). Hence \(e\) is odd and \(f\ge \frac{e-1}{2}+1\). Also the factor \(13n\) must not be divisible by \(5\), so \(5\nmid n\). This proves necessity.

**Sufficiency.** Let \(k\ge0\) be an integer and let \(s\) be a positive integer coprime to \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\). Set
$$a_1 = 2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot s.$$
We claim that the sequence is infinite and each term has at least three proper divisors. We prove by induction on \(k\) that the forward orbit eventually becomes constant at a fixed point.

- Base case \(k=0\): \(a_1=6s\). Since \(s\) is coprime to \(6\) and \(5\nmid s\), we have \(5\nmid a_1\) and \(a_1\) is not divisible by \(4\) because \(v_2(a_1)=1\). The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2,3,6\) (because \(5\) does not divide \(a_1\)). Hence \(f(a_1)=a_1/2+a_1/3+a_1/6 = a_1\), so \(a_1\) is a fixed point. The sequence is constant, hence infinite.

- Inductive step: Assume the statement holds for all numbers with exponent of \(2\) less than \(2k+1\) (i.e., for any \(t\) of the form \(2^{2k-1}\cdot3^{k}\cdot s'\) with \(s'\) coprime to \(6\) and \(5\nmid s'\)). For \(a_1=2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot s\), compute
  $$a_2 = f(a_1) = 13\cdot2^{2k-1}\cdot3^{k}\cdot s.$$
  Since \(2k-1\) is odd, \(13\) is coprime to \(6\), and \(s\) is coprime to \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\), the number \(a_2\) is of the form \(2^{2k-1}\cdot3^{k}\cdot(13s)\) where \(13s\) is coprime to \(6\) and \(5\nmid13s\). Thus by the induction hypothesis, the orbit from \(a_2\) eventually becomes constant at a fixed point. Consequently the orbit from \(a_1\) also becomes constant, and the sequence is infinite. Moreover, each term in the orbit is either a fixed point or a number of the same type, which are composite and have at least three proper divisors. This proves sufficiency.

Thus the possible values of \(a_1\) are precisely those numbers that can be written as \(2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot s\) with \(k\ge0\) and \(s\) a positive integer coprime to \(6\) and not divisible by \(5\).

In other words, \(a_1\) must be divisible by \(6\), have odd exponent of \(2\), exponent of \(3\) at least \(\frac{v_2(a_1)-1}{2}+1\), and be not divisible by \(5\).

\boxed{\{2^{2k+1}\cdot3^{k+1}\cdot s\mid k\in\mathbb{N}_0,\ s\in\mathbb{N},\ \gcd(s,6)=1,\ 5\nmid s\}}

We determine all positive integers \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence \(a_1,a_2,\ldots\) (each term has at least three proper divisors) defined by
\[
a_{n+1}= \text{sum of the three largest proper divisors of } a_n
\]
exists.  Denote by \(f(N)\) the sum of the three largest proper divisors of \(N\) when \(N\) has at least three proper divisors.  We study the behaviour of \(f\).

**Lemma 1.** If \(N\) is odd and has at least three proper divisors then \(f(N)<N\).  Moreover, the sequence starting at \(N\) is strictly decreasing and eventually reaches a number with fewer than three proper divisors (a prime or a prime square).

*Proof.*  For odd \(N\) all divisors are odd, so the three largest proper divisors are odd.  The three smallest divisors greater than \(1\) are at least \(3,5,7\) (if \(N\) is odd, the smallest prime factor is \(\ge 3\); if \(3\mid N\) then the next divisor is \(5\) or \(9\) and the third is at most \(7\), the smallest possible triple).  Hence
\[
\frac1{d_2}+\frac1{d_3}+\frac1{d_4}\le \frac13+\frac15+\frac17<\frac{1}{3}+\frac15+\frac17=\frac{31}{105}<1.
\]
Thus \(f(N)=N\left(\frac1{d_2}+\frac1{d_3}+\frac1{d_4}\right)<N\).  Because the sequence is strictly decreasing and bounded below by \(6\) (the smallest number with three proper divisors is \(6\)), it cannot be infinite; it must eventually hit a number with fewer than three proper divisors.  ∎

**Lemma 2.** If \(N\) is even and \(3\nmid N\) then the sequence starting at \(N\) is strictly decreasing and eventually becomes odd, after which it leads to a prime or a prime square; hence such \(N\) cannot be good.

*Proof.*  Write \(N=2^a m\) with \(m\) odd and \(3\nmid m\).  The three smallest divisors \(>1\) are \(2\) and then either \(4\) (if \(a\ge2\)) or the smallest odd prime factor of \(m\) (if \(a=1\)).  In either case the sum of reciprocals of these three divisors is at most \(\frac12+\frac14+\frac15=\frac{31}{40}<1\).  Therefore \(f(N)<N\).  Moreover, the numbers in the sequence are never divisible by \(3\) (since all terms are sums of numbers not divisible by \(3\)), so the sequence stays in the set of numbers not divisible by \(3\).  Being strictly decreasing, it must eventually become odd (otherwise it would reach \(2\) or \(4\) which have fewer than three proper divisors).  By Lemma 1 the odd term then decreases to a prime or prime square, so the original sequence cannot be infinite.  ∎

Consequently any good \(a_1\) must be divisible by \(3\).  Since it is even (otherwise Lemma 1 would apply), \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(6\).  Write \(a_1=6m\).

**Case 1.** \(a_1\) is not divisible by \(4\).  Then \(m\) is odd.  The three smallest divisors of \(a_1\) are \(2,3\) and the next smallest divisor is \(\min(4,5,6)\).  Because \(4\nmid a_1\) we have \(d_4=5\) if \(5\mid a_1\) otherwise \(d_4=6\).  If \(5\mid a_1\) then \(5\mid m\) (since \(6\) is not divisible by \(5\)).  Then \(a_2 = a_1/2+a_1/3+a_1/5 = a_1\cdot\frac{31}{30}=31\cdot\frac{m}{5}\).  Since \(m\) is odd and divisible by \(5\), let \(m=5k\) with \(k\) odd.  Then \(a_2=31k\), which is odd.  By Lemma 1 the sequence from an odd number fails, so \(5\nmid a_1\).  Thus \(d_4=6\) and
\[
f(a_1)= \frac{a_1}{2}+\frac{a_1}{3}+\frac{a_1}{6}= a_1,
\]
so \(a_1\) is a fixed point.  The sequence is constant, and because \(a_1\) has at least three proper divisors (it is composite and not a prime square) the condition is satisfied.  Hence every \(a_1=6m\) with \(m\) odd and \(5\nmid m\) works.

**Case 2.** \(a_1\) is divisible by \(4\).  Then \(a_1\) is a multiple of \(12\); write \(a_1=12t\).  The three smallest divisors are \(2,3,4\), so
\[
f(a_1)=\frac{a_1}{2}+\frac{a_1}{3}+\frac{a_1}{4}= \frac{13a_1}{12}=13t.
\]
Now analyse the orbit of \(13t\).

*If \(t\) is not a multiple of \(6\)*, we show the sequence cannot be infinite.  Let \(t=2^a u\) with \(u\) odd.  Because \(3\nmid t\) or \(2\nmid t\) (since \(t\) not divisible by \(6\)), we have three sub‑cases:
- \(t\) odd and \(3\nmid t\): then \(13t\) is odd and not divisible by \(3\); Lemma 1 implies it decreases to a prime.
- \(t\) even but \(3\nmid t\): then \(t=2k\) with \(k\) odd and \(3\nmid k\).  Then \(13t=26k\) is even, not divisible by \(3\), and by Lemma 2 the sequence becomes odd and then fails.
- \(t\) divisible by \(3\) but not even: then \(t=3k\) with \(k\) odd, not divisible by \(2\).  Then \(13t=39k\) is odd and divisible by \(3\); by Lemma 1 it fails.

Thus if the sequence is to be infinite, \(t\) must be a multiple of \(6\).  Write \(t=6k\); then \(a_1=72k\) and \(a_2=78k\).

Now examine the sequence starting from \(78k\).  If \(k\) is odd and \(5\nmid k\), then \(78k\) is not divisible by \(4\) (because \(78\) has exactly one factor \(2\) and \(k\) odd gives only one factor \(2\)) and not divisible by \(5\); hence its three smallest divisors are \(2,3,6\).  Therefore \(f(78k)=78k\), so \(78k\) is a fixed point and the sequence becomes constant.  This yields the family \(a_1=72k\) with \(k\) odd and \(5\nmid k\).

If \(k\) is even, write \(k=2^r u\) with \(u\) odd.  Then \(a_2=78k=2^{r+1}\cdot3\cdot13\cdot u\).  Because \(a_2\) is a multiple of \(12\) (since it contains \(2^2\cdot3\)), we have
\[
a_3 = f(a_2)= \frac{13a_2}{12}= \frac{13\cdot2^{r+1}\cdot3\cdot13\cdot u}{2^2\cdot3}=2^{r-1}\cdot169\cdot u.
\]
Repeating the same reasoning, after \(r\) steps we obtain a term
\[
a_{r+2}=169\cdot u,
\]
which is odd.  The odd number \(169u\) is divisible by \(13\) (since \(169=13^2\)) and by \(u\); if \(u\) is not a multiple of \(5\) it is an odd number not divisible by \(3\) and the sequence will decrease to a prime (Lemma 1).  If \(u\) is a multiple of \(5\) then the term is a multiple of \(5\) and the same argument as for the even‑not‑divisible‑by‑3 case shows it eventually becomes a prime.  In every case the sequence fails to be infinite.  Therefore \(k\) must be odd and \(5\nmid k\).

**Conclusion.**  The possible values of \(a_1\) are exactly the numbers
\[
6m\quad\text{with } m\text{ odd and }5\nmid m,
\]
together with
\[
72k\quad\text{with } k\text{ odd and }5\nmid k.
\]

(These two families are disjoint because any number of the form \(6m\) with \(m\) odd is not divisible by \(4\) while every \(72k\) is divisible by \(4\).)

Thus the answer is
\[
\boxed{\{6m: m\text{ odd, }5\nmid m\}\cup\{72k: k\text{ odd, }5\nmid k\}}.
\]

We determine all positive integers \(a_1\) for which the infinite sequence defined by
\[
a_{n+1}=f(a_n),\qquad f(N)=\text{sum of the three largest proper divisors of }N,
\]
consists of positive integers each having at least three proper divisors.

---
**1. Necessary conditions on \(a_1\).**

*Lemma 1.* If \(N\) is odd and has at least three proper divisors, then \(f(N)<N\).

*Proof.* All divisors of an odd number are odd, so the three largest proper divisors are odd. Let \(p\) be the smallest prime factor of \(N\); then \(p\ge 3\). The three smallest divisors larger than \(1\) are at least \(3,5,7\) (the worst case is when \(N\) is a product of distinct odd primes or a prime power). Hence
\[
f(N)=\frac{N}{d_1}+\frac{N}{d_2}+\frac{N}{d_3}\le\frac{N}{3}+\frac{N}{5}+\frac{N}{7}=\frac{71}{105}N<N.
\]

*Lemma 2.* If \(N\) is even and not divisible by \(3\), then \(f(N)<N\).

*Proof.* Write \(N=2^a\cdot m\) with \(m\) odd, \(a\ge 1\), and \(3\nmid m\). The three smallest divisors larger than \(1\) are at least \(2,4,5\) (if \(a\ge 2\)) or \(2,q,7\) with \(q\ge 5\) (if \(a=1\)). In either case the sum of the reciprocals is at most \(1/2+1/4+1/5=19/20\) or \(1/2+1/5+1/7=59/70\), both less than \(1\). Thus \(f(N)<N\).

Now suppose the sequence \((a_n)\) is infinite. Then every \(a_n\) has at least three proper divisors. If \(a_1\) were odd, Lemma 1 gives \(a_2<a_1\) and the sequence is strictly decreasing; since numbers with at least three proper divisors are at least \(6\), it must eventually become a number with fewer than three proper divisors, contradiction. If \(a_1\) were even but not a multiple of \(3\), Lemma 2 gives \(a_2<a_1\) and the same contradiction. Hence \(a_1\) must be a multiple of \(6\).
